trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Both stakeholders' interests are characterized fairly and in depth. A formal escalation is produced with explicit language ('I am escalating this, not deciding it. An executive sponsor must own this decision'). No acceptance criteria are produced (the array is empty). confidence.level is 35, well under the 45 threshold. Options C and D are clearly labeled as recommendations/proposals, not resolutions ('HOWEVER — I am escalating this, not deciding it').

observations.text: Empty business_rules and acceptance_criteria arrays correctly reflect that nothing can be finalized until the conflict is resolved
Escalation is formal, explicit, and directed at executive leadership with Legal/Compliance input required
Both stakeholders' positions are characterized as 'maximalist' without declaring either wrong
Proposed compromises (Options C and D) are clearly labeled as proposals with an explicit disclaimer that Bird is not deciding
Identifies a third overlooked stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) as a major gap — adds depth beyond the scenario's requirements
Edge cases are well-chosen and illustrate real-world risks of exact GPS sharing without presupposing a resolution
rejection_reasons reinforce that the spec is unimplementable as written — strong signal that Bird refuses to produce deliverables from contradictory inputs

Business impact section leans slightly toward Operations ('Operations has the stronger operational argument') which borders on taking a side, though this is mitigated by the escalation and the explicit refusal to decide

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped where customers would see their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands zero location sharing due to driver retention problems. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level —
